data.title: As the sergeant laughed that encryption was dead, the sergeant was traversing that damned cycle.
data.description:
Author: Haotian

“The superior man, when he hears of the Way, diligently practices it; the average man, when he hears of the Way, is half-convinced, half-doubtful; the inferior man, when he hears of the Way, laughs loudly at it. -- *Tao Te Ching*

This is a sentence that suddenly came to mind recently amidst the noise of “Anything But Crypto,” “Meme Nihilism,” and “Crypto Doomsday.”

This feeling has become even stronger after reading Vitalik Buterin’s interview about 2026.

We see Vitalik Buterin sitting relaxed on a swing in Chiang Mai, not ecstatic about the progress of Ethereum’s technology roadmap, but instead seeing a huge gap between technology and application.

Why? Ethereum has made great progress in scaling technology, Gas…

Limits have doubled, zkEVM has been successfully implemented, and user experience, including account abstraction, has significantly improved. Technically, we've won big, but in application, we've lost miserably. In other words, we've built a supercar (L2/L3) capable of 300 km/h, but the roads are full of "old man's cars" (memes) and "bumper cars" (PVP battles). This strong sense of "dislocation" is incomprehensible to most people. Drawing on Vitalik's reflections, I want to strip away the grand narrative and discuss these four harsh truths that only "sergeants" care about. When "infrastructure" becomes an addictive dependency, we fall into a collective illusion: we not only need to build roads, but more roads, faster roads, even if there are no cars on them. We initially envisioned a "decentralized Uber" or a "Web3 Amazon," but what we've gotten is endless financial speculation. This is a classic example of a structural mismatch between "infrastructure overcapacity" and "extreme PMF (product-market fit) scarcity." Why is this? The answer is simply that "selling shovels" is easier to tell a story about than "digging for gold." In the past few years, VCs and developers have conspired to stage a frenzy of "technological narcissism." As long as your TPS is high enough, and your ZK algorithm is obscure enough, you can get a high valuation. Whether anyone uses it? It doesn't matter; that's for the next cycle. In reality, true technology should be like air and water—you depend on it for survival, yet you don't feel its presence. The hype surrounding infrastructure must shift to applications and services. If the problem of "who will drive" isn't solved, building more roads is just erecting tombstones for the industry's desolation. The Essence of Social Interaction: Don't Let Dopamine Kill Oxytocin

Vitalik made a profound statement: Financial incentives that are too strong will directly destroy the essence of social interaction.

When we introduced Tokenomics into social products, we thought we were incentivizing users, but in reality, we were feeding greed. Was the early growth data social? No, it was a frenzy for "on-chain gold farming studios." Once expected returns declined and the crowd dispersed, it left a mess.

This is why Vitalik affirmed the strategic wisdom of Farcaster's transformation into a wallet.

In the era of AI agents and high-frequency interactions, the wallet is the "universal connector" that connects everything. When Farcaster decided to embed social relationships at the wallet's underlying layer, rather than simply creating a "Web3 version of Twitter," it wasn't a surrender or retreat, but rather a pinpointing of the lifeline of Crypto products.

data.title: TikTok Agrees to Settle Case Claiming Teen Addiction and Depression While Facing Surge in App Deletions
data.description:
The Digital Addiction Trial That Is Changing Everything

A 19-year-old Californian known as K.G.M. just walked to the edge of a legal cliff and watched two of the world’s biggest tech giants blink.

On 27 January 2026, TikTok reached a settlement in principle with the young woman just hours before jury selection was set to begin in Los Angeles.

This move follows a similar confidential agreement made by Snap on 20 January 2026.

TIKTOK SETTLES: TikTok agrees to settle major lawsuit accusing social media giants of designing addictive platforms that harm children — just before jury selection in Los Angeles.@Snapchat parent Snap settled last week; @Meta and @Google now face trial that could reshape tech… pic.twitter.com/1w9P7Mcfqh

— The Dallas Express News (@DallasExpress) January 27, 2026

While the courtrooms of the California Superior Court were prepared for a full-scale battle over the mental health of a generation, the focus has now shifted to the two remaining titans in the room: Meta and YouTube.

The case brought by K.G.M. is not just another lawsuit; it is a bellwether trial, a legal test case that could dictate the fate of hundreds of other families.

At the heart of her claim is a harrowing journey through depression and suicidal thoughts, which she attributes to the attention-grabbing design of these apps.

KGM, a 19-year-old from the United States, claims her addiction to Meta, Google and ByteDance platforms at an early age left her with suicidal thoughts and depression.
➡️ https://t.co/Gldmi6VHaxpic.twitter.com/xUgdkZA6s2

— euronews (@euronews) January 28, 2026

As she seeks to hold these companies accountable for their role in a youth mental health crisis, the industry is watching closely.

While TikTok and Snap have stepped back from the immediate fire of this specific trial, Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg is still expected to take the stand, marking a high-stakes moment for the future of social media regulation.

Why Did TikTok Settle Now?

The timing of TikTok's decision has raised eyebrows across the tech sector.

Joseph VanZandt, K.G.M.’s lawyer, stated,

"Plaintiff KGM and defendant TikTok have reached an agreement in principle to settle her case."

The details remain a closely guarded secret, leaving experts to wonder if the settlement was a strategic move to avoid a public airing of internal documents or a sympathetic victim in front of a jury.

Eric Goldman, a professor at Santa Clara University School of Law, noted that without knowing the terms, it is impossible to jump to conclusions.

He explained,

"We don’t know if any money moved. We don’t know if any remedial changes were agreed to.”

This legal pivot comes at a chaotic moment for TikTok.

The company only recently finalised a new ownership structure in the United States, yet it is already battling claims of censorship and massive technical failures.

A power outage at a U.S. data centre partner on Monday left creators staring at zero views on their videos and caused widespread timeout errors.

Since yesterday we’ve been working to restore our services following a power outage at a U.S. data center impacting TikTok and other apps we operate. We're working with our data center partner to stabilize our service. We're sorry for this disruption and hope to resolve it soon.

— TikTok USDS Joint Venture (@tiktokusdsjv) January 26, 2026

While TikTok’s Jamie Favazza insisted these were purely "technical issues we’ve transparently confirmed," some users remain unconvinced.

The friction has led to a 150 per cent surge in app uninstalls over a five-day period, though paradoxically, daily average users still managed a 2 per cent climb.

BREAKING: 📱 TikTok is seeing a mass exodus in the U.S.

Uninstalls have surged nearly 150% in the past five days as users delete the app in large numbers. pic.twitter.com/94UZu1S3QN

— War Pulse (@War_Pulse24) January 28, 2026

Will Meta And Google Stand Their Ground?

Unlike their counterparts, Meta and Alphabet’s YouTube show no signs of backing down.

Mark Lanier, another attorney for K.G.M., confirmed that "at this point, there’s no dialogue for settlement with Google or with Meta."

Google has explicitly stated they are not in talks, and Meta has remained silent.

These companies continue to argue that federal law protects them from liability regarding user-posted content and that there is insufficient evidence to prove their designs are intentionally addictive.

Mark Lanier, the lead plaintiff's lawyer in the lawsuit alleging Instagram and YouTube intentionally addict young people, spoke to reporters after court today.

"We've got the documents where internally they're worried that they're the same as Big Tobacco with their addictive… pic.twitter.com/ukUSNQNiA5

— Meghann Cuniff (@meghanncuniff) January 28, 2026

The financial stakes are astronomical.

Bloomberg Intelligence analyst Matthew Schettenhelm suggested that the industry "likely can’t avoid paying hundreds of millions of dollars — and potentially far more," with total exposure potentially reaching into the billions.

Beyond this single case, there are 2,500 personal injury suits, 1,000 public school district lawsuits, and dozens of state attorneys general waiting in the wings.

As the trial moves forward against the remaining defendants, lead attorneys Lexi Hazam and Previn Warren believe more evidence will emerge regarding "widespread harm caused by social media platforms that put profit over adolescent safety."

Can Algorithms Truly Be Held Liable?

The proceedings in Los Angeles are entering a delicate phase.

Judge Carolyn Kuhl has already warned prospective jurors that while they aren't required to stop using social media, they must not investigate platform features they don't normally use.

This highlights the difficulty of finding an impartial jury in a world where almost everyone is a user of the products being sued.

If K.G.M. succeeds in her remaining claims, it could force a fundamental redesign of how algorithms function, moving away from infinite scrolls and dopamine-heavy notifications that critics say have hooked a generation.

Are We Trading Mental Health For Digital Convenience

Coinlive views the current legal landscape as a necessary, if uncomfortable, audit of our digital existence.

For years, the "attention economy" has operated without a seatbelt, treating user engagement as a metric of success while ignoring the psychological cost to the developing brain.

If these platforms are forced to pay out billions, it shouldn't just be viewed as a legal loss, but as a long-overdue "safety tax" on products that were released into the wild without sufficient guardrails.

Society is finally asking whether the convenience of a personalized feed is worth the systematic erosion of youth mental health.

Moving forward, the real victory won’t be found in confidential settlements, but in whether these companies are forced to redesign their core architecture to value human well-being over the infinite scroll.

data.title: How to prevent cryptocurrencies from ever reaching a $10 trillion market capitalization?
data.description:
Author: Arjun Chand Source: Bankless Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Charlie Munger often says, "Think the other way around, always think the other way around." Instead of figuring out how to succeed, think about how to fail. Here are some reliable ways to ensure that cryptocurrency never becomes a $10 trillion market.

Taking it for granted that the whole world cares about our public chain

Since the birth of cryptocurrency, the entire industry has revolved around public chains. For a long time, this direction was undeniable. Public chains are the fundamental foundation for all applications, so it is crucial to build this foundation well, and we should invest all our energy, talent, and capital into building top-tier public chains.

And that's exactly what we did.

In the first 15 years of cryptocurrency development, public chains became the most funded sector in the industry.

From Satoshi Nakamoto to Vitalik Buterin and Anatoly, the industry's top talents have dedicated their prime years to building public blockchains according to their own visions, optimizing what they consider to be the core features. This model of building an ecosystem around a public blockchain has had a massive, ripple-like impact on all aspects of the industry. For a long time, the market has believed that value will concentrate at the public blockchain layer, a view widely disseminated due to the "fat protocol theory." Public blockchain tokens are among the best-performing assets in cryptocurrency history. Over the past decade, from Ethereum to SOL to BNB, these tokens have delivered exceptionally high returns to investors. Wallets have become the most universal interaction portal in the industry; the core design of these tools is to help users interact with the on-chain ecosystem. Developers and users have begun to gather around public blockchains, gravitating towards ecosystems that align with their own philosophies and establishing long-term commitments. This loyalty has led to user retention, but it has also fostered divisions within communities and challenges in collaboration. This positioning and operating model once propelled us forward smoothly. In the early stages of cryptocurrency development, the industry needed an anchor, something tangible that fostered a sense of belonging, and public blockchains became that anchor. They became the standard-bearers of different cryptocurrency philosophies, with each public blockchain optimizing through different trade-offs. The results are evident: the cryptocurrency market is now worth $4 trillion, and building public blockchains has become one of the most profitable businesses in the industry. This trend has further expanded the cryptocurrency market, giving institutions the incentive to issue their own public blockchains and enter the ecosystem. All of this seemed reasonable at the time. But now, it should also mark the beginning of the end for the idea that "cryptocurrencies must revolve around public blockchains." Because now, this logic is beginning to backfire on the industry, hindering our development. The reason behind this is actually quite simple: fragmentation, nothing more. The fragmentation of liquidity, capital, users, talent, and attention. Under the "public chain-first" mindset, our industry's core is fragmented, with each ecosystem developing in isolation. We often feel that different sectors within the industry are independent, as if the dynamics of one area of ​​cryptocurrency won't directly or indirectly affect other sectors. However, the reality is that cryptocurrency is a whole composed of a cluster of closely connected small markets, collectively forming a unified global market. Any form of fragmentation makes the entire system more fragile. Consider a question once posed by account X in the a16z cryptocurrency forum: "Which public chain are you developing on?" This precisely exposes the core problem of this public chain mindset. Does it matter whether developers are developing on Ethereum, Solana, or some unknown public chain? What truly matters is that they are developing for the cryptocurrency ecosystem. This is inherently a unified market. However, precisely because we define cryptocurrency as a collection of independent public blockchains, resources are consequently dispersed. Liquidity, capital, users, and developers are spread across different ecosystems, each optimizing its own "walled garden." This localized optimization ultimately sacrifices the overall development of the entire industry. This is precisely the reverse thinking the entire industry needs to adopt: We must stop building for a single public blockchain and start building for the entire cryptocurrency market. Otherwise, we risk "optimizing individuals while stifling overall market growth." Human society has a simple and ancient example that can offer us insight—the nation-state. A nation may be divided into different states to improve local governance efficiency, but on the global stage, these states compete and operate as a unified economy. All states working together to form one nation – this is the most economically sound model: one voice, one vision, one shared goal. No mature nation would sacrifice the national market to optimize the development of its states. The cryptocurrency industry also needs such a moment of unity. Public blockchains are the states, and cryptocurrency is the nation. Although we are divided into different "public blockchain states," we belong to the same great nation of "cryptocurrency" – we must unite to make cryptocurrency a larger market. Focusing on the market, not public blockchains, should be the battle cry for the future of cryptocurrency. From the outside, cryptocurrency is inherently a unified whole. When institutions, fintech companies, governments, or consumer platforms evaluate cryptocurrencies, they don't see individual public blockchains, but only a massive, continuously growing market. In other words, they see a huge market opportunity. If we want to usher in the next wave of adoption, we must embrace this reality and be willing to set aside internal interests, community loyalty, and preferences for held tokens. This means we need to clearly explain to the world why cryptocurrency as a whole represents a tremendous opportunity: it can rebuild financial infrastructure on-chain, creating a globally accessible financial network based on a transparent public ledger, enabling faster and lower-cost value transfer for everyone. Through this effort, cryptocurrency can unlock entirely new financial primitives, attract new users, foster new business models, and create greater value for all participants in the global financial system—something structurally impossible with traditional financial systems. Focus on product development and wait for users to come. One of the most dismissive criticisms of cryptocurrency is that it "has never created what people truly need." I disagree with this statement. This argument has now become a consensus, simply because it's easy and perfunctory to dismiss cryptocurrencies as "not producing anything truly remarkable." The reality is that this criticism started with flawed expectations and used the wrong thinking model. It assumes that cryptocurrencies should give rise to social applications for ordinary consumers, ignoring the fact that cryptocurrencies have been rebuilding the global financial system from scratch. Therefore, it's inevitable that our applications will inherently possess financial and speculative attributes. Perhaps cryptocurrencies will eventually give rise to phenomenal social applications, or perhaps not; this remains an open question. We've seen many attempts: for example, Farcaster, initially a social network, has recently transformed into a "wallet with social feeds"; and emerging platforms like Base App and Zora blur the boundaries between content, identity, and funds. But ultimately, no cryptocurrency application can escape one core element—its financial nature. I believe this is precisely where the true innovation of cryptocurrency lies. Cryptocurrency embeds the ability to transfer value directly into the underlying layer of the internet. This is something the world has always lacked, and we are changing that. Don't let anyone tell you otherwise. Any cryptocurrency application will embody this characteristic in some form. Denying this is underestimating what the industry has already achieved. The cryptocurrency field has already given birth to many impressive products, and negative reviews arise simply because these products are either financial primitives or more or less related to speculation. But on this point, I want to point out: the entire world operates without speculation, and cryptocurrency is rebuilding the traditional financial system from scratch with entirely new principles. Therefore, it is not surprising that our applications cater to this behavior. Admittedly, it is easy for people to categorize these applications as "tools that fuel speculation" and claim that "cryptocurrency is nothing but a casino." However, speculation is not a unique attribute of cryptocurrencies; rather, it is a universal operating logic across the entire world and all global markets. Therefore, it is not surprising that the most common applications of cryptocurrencies today include trading terminals, exchange front-ends, leverage platforms, and meme markets. To outsiders, this may seem like financial nihilism, but in reality, it is simply cryptocurrencies fulfilling real needs in the world. In this process, we have created entirely new markets: memes transform attention and online culture into tradable assets; prediction markets provide a channel for people to speculate on global events; non-fungible tokens (NFGs) create native digital forms for art and ownership; decentralized finance (DeFi) has broken free from credit scoring systems, rebuilt lending businesses, and replaced the old reputation system with collateral and algorithms. But today, the real challenge facing the industry is not application development, but channel reach. Previously, the growth logic of the cryptocurrency industry was: build better technology and then wait for users to come. But that phase is over. From now on, industry growth will depend on our ability to reach those who were previously uninterested in cryptocurrencies. Therefore, I say the cryptocurrency industry now faces the challenge of channel reach. Our problem seems simple, yet it's difficult to solve: how do we promote cryptocurrencies to people who never use cryptocurrency Twitter? Currently, cryptocurrency marketing is almost entirely inward-looking; frankly, it's just self-indulgence by insiders. We're talking to developers, traders, and core users on cryptocurrency Twitter, thinking we're educating the market. But in reality, we're just repeatedly indoctrinating the same audience. We have no idea how to reach people outside the cryptocurrency Twitter bubble, and I'm part of the problem; I don't know the answer either. I believe we need channels that already reach the mainstream. This statement might draw criticism from many, but centralized exchanges possess advantages such as mainstream access, user trust, and familiarity, making them perhaps the best choice for promoting cryptocurrency popularization: They already enjoy national-level brand recognition; they have ample revenue and marketing budgets to reach the mainstream audience—not just native cryptocurrency Twitter users; their products cater to the needs of ordinary investors, precisely addressing the pain points of most people by providing what users want (crypto functionality and earning opportunities) and making it easy to access these. Consider this: How many people outside the cryptocurrency community know Uniswap? Very few. How many people outside the cryptocurrency community know Binance or Coinbase? Quite a few. Therefore, centralized exchanges might become the next entry point for users, though I cannot be certain. I also don't want to put all my eggs in one basket with centralized exchanges, because they are ultimately a compromise, not a completely native product of cryptocurrency. However, the success of centralized exchanges offers a key lesson for the cryptocurrency industry's growth: we must prioritize accessibility and promote cryptocurrencies globally in simple, straightforward language. We must build trustworthy brands and market to ordinary investors, not just those within the cryptocurrency community. From now on, the success of cryptocurrencies will depend less on superior protocols and more on effective dissemination. We need to think like marketers, not just developers. Therefore, solving the challenge of cryptocurrency accessibility and conveying its value to the world is everyone's responsibility. We cannot sit idly by; if we want to promote the adoption of cryptocurrencies, we must make the world understand, want, and have access to them, actively attracting users instead of waiting for them to come to us. Jim, I want to talk to you about cryptocurrency. An anonymous person named Satoshi Nakamoto started it all, and then someone named Vitalik Buterin made it programmable. Now, anyone with internet access and a cryptocurrency wallet can transfer funds globally. The market is open 24/7, you can trade anytime—memes, prediction markets, stocks, you name it. And those traditional financial giants in suits are thrown into disarray; they all say this is the future of finance.

Selling out core values ​​to traditional financial giants

There's a shortcut to making cryptocurrencies fail completely: surrender to those suited traditional financial giants just before reaching the finish line.

It took 15 years for cryptocurrencies to gain the attention they deserve, and now we inevitably have an impulse to compromise in order to secure partnerships with institutions, softening our stance in the name of pragmatism to cater to the current market situation.

And this will be the biggest mistake we may make.

As institutions enter the on-chain ecosystem, the cryptocurrency industry needs to uphold the core values ​​that truly give it value: self-custody, censorship resistance, permissionless access, and open participation.

Today, cryptocurrencies have already secured their place, and the worst thing to do is to treat these core principles as bargaining chips. If we do that, we will ultimately fail. Because once we strip away the inherent advantages of cryptocurrencies, we can only compete with them according to the rules of traditional institutions, which is destined to be a defeat. This misguided approach has several obvious manifestations. Take stablecoins as an example; they are inherently a superior way of transferring value on the internet: faster settlement, lower costs, and global coverage. Viewing central bank digital currencies as a reasonable alternative completely deviates from the core meaning of stablecoins. Looking at private blockchains, they are often touted as a "reasonable compromise" to attract institutional participation, but in reality, this is nothing more than a mediocre compromise, pleasing neither side. It is neither true cryptocurrency nor traditional finance; it is merely a product caught in the middle, offering no real value to anyone. Private blockchains sacrifice the transparency and composability of cryptocurrencies, and these are not compromises we should be making. If institutions want to establish themselves in the cryptocurrency ecosystem, they should create products that align with the core principles of cryptocurrency. Cryptocurrencies no longer need to prove their worth; that debate is over. Now, what we need to do is protect the core qualities that initially made them worthy of acceptance. In the final sprint, there is no room for compromise. Conclusion If cryptocurrencies want to forever remain below a $10 trillion market capitalization, the method is actually quite clear: Focusing on public chains, not the market—this is the best way to maintain a closed-door approach. Splitting into individual "walled gardens"—this is the best way to maintain a small market size. Building only applications without solving channel access issues—this is the best way to keep the product forever at the theoretical level. Pushing complex operations onto users—this is the best way to completely lose users. Abandoning the core concept— This is a perfect way to sell out core values ​​under the guise of development. We shouldn't go down this dead end, but instead focus our energy on the right things: The market is more important than public chains; applications are more important than infrastructure; making the cryptocurrency market accessible to everyone is paramount; and we must firmly uphold the core qualities that make cryptocurrency what it is. Cryptocurrencies don't need miracles to reach a $10 trillion market capitalization; they just need to stop doing things that hinder their progress. Reverse these flawed practices, and the market will provide the rest. After all, that's how the market has always been.

data.title: Behind Tether's $15 billion fortune: From USDT issuer to shadow bank
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

January 27, 2026, according to Fortune, Tether achieved a profit of approximately $15 billion in 2025. Tether's holdings of US Treasury bonds have exceeded those of large economies like South Korea, and it also has significant holdings in Bitcoin and gold.

I. What does a profit of $15 billion mean?

What does $15 billion mean? Perhaps it's better understood by comparing it to the GDP of tech giants and national economies. Tether's 2025 profit is equivalent to 1/4 of Amazon and 1/7.5 of Apple. Even among tech giants that have been highly profitable over the past 10-15 years, Tether's profit is roughly equivalent to the annual profit of a medium-sized tech company.

If compared at the level of national GDP, Tether's 2025 profits are equivalent to half of Iceland's or the entirety of Laos's. If we consider $15 billion as its "size," then Tether is roughly equivalent to a small economy ranked around 40-60th in global GDP (such as some Eastern European, African, or small-to-medium-sized Asian countries). In summary, Tether's annual profit as a stablecoin company is roughly equivalent to the annual economic output of a medium-sized developing country.

...

II. How did Tether earn $15 billion?

1. Core Profit Logic: US Treasury Bonds

Tether hasn't always been this profitable; such a large profit scale is a recent phenomenon, occurring only in the last two years. In 2023, Tether's net profit was $6.2 billion, soaring to $13 billion in 2024, and setting a new record of $15 billion in 2025.

II. How did Tether earn $15 billion?

1. Core Profit Logic: US Treasury Bonds

Tether hasn't always been this profitable; such a large profit scale is a recent development. In 2023, Tether's net profit was $6.2 billion, soaring to $13 billion in 2024, and reaching a new record of $15 billion in 2025.

Tether's main source of income is not transaction fees, nor the issuance of stablecoins themselves, but rather the interest generated by its reserve assets.

As of the past two years, Tether's reserve structure has been comprised of over 70% U.S. Treasury bonds and similar highly liquid assets, with the remainder consisting of precious metals and Bitcoin.

According to the "Tether (USDT) January 2026 Reserve Report," as of the end of 2025, Tether (USDT) reserves reached over $181 billion, of which over $174 billion was in circulation, with approximately $135 billion backed by U.S. Treasury bonds, gold, Bitcoin, and excess buffer assets.

Tether's reserves are primarily allocated to low-risk, highly liquid assets, with approximately 77% in cash equivalents (mainly U.S. Treasury bills and related instruments). This structure supports the stability of USDT despite its ever-increasing circulating supply, which reached approximately $186.91 billion as of January 18, 2026. Tether's strategy emphasizes both security and returns, generating over $10 billion in profits in the first nine months of 2025, a portion of which was reinvested in assets such as Bitcoin.

Specifically, Tether's exposure to U.S. Treasury bonds (direct and indirect) is approximately $135 billion to $138 billion, making it one of the world's largest holders of U.S. government debt. It ranks around 17th globally in U.S. Treasury holdings. Other diversified investments include gold (7.13%) and Bitcoin (5.44%), enhancing its resilience. The asset composition backing Tether's USD stablecoin is shown below. The period from 2023 to 2025 falls within a rare high-interest-rate cycle. US short-term Treasury yields have long remained above 4%–5%. If we calculate based on holding $135 billion in US Treasury bonds with a yield of 5%, then Tether's annualized interest on these bonds would be approximately $6.75 billion. Unlike traditional banks that pay interest on deposits, Tether does not pay interest to USDT holders. Therefore, it can invest this "liability (USDT)" in high-yield assets to profit from the interest rate spread. This is Tether's core profit-making logic. 2. Riding the Wave: Gold Hoarding Recently, influenced by factors such as geopolitical risks, gold prices have soared. As of press time, spot gold has reached a new high, breaking through $5200/ounce for the first time, with a cumulative increase of over $880 in the first month of the new year, a 20% increase. Over the past year, Tether has targeted the gold market, quietly becoming one of the largest participants in the global gold market. Tether CEO Paul Aldoino has likened the company's role in the gold market to that of a central bank and predicted that Washington's geopolitical rivals will introduce gold-backed alternatives to the dollar. He also revealed that the company plans to continue investing huge profits in the gold market while beginning to compete with banks in gold trading. "We will soon become one of the world's largest gold reserve central banks." Tether purchased more than 70 tons of gold last year. This figure exceeds the declared purchases of almost any central bank: only Poland's increase (102 tons) surpassed Tether's. Aldoino stated that Tether will continue to purchase at a rate of 1-2 tons per week and intends to continue doing so in the coming months. The proportion of gold in Tether's reserves has increased. Why does Aldoino favor gold? "Logically, gold is safer than any national currency. Every central bank in the BRICS countries is buying gold. Tether's USD stablecoin users in emerging markets are those who love gold and have been using it to hedge against their governments' long-term currency devaluation. We believe the world is heading towards darkness. We believe the world is unstable." But is Tether simply hoarding gold for safety? No.

Tether also hopes to be able to trade gold—competing with JPMorgan Chase, HSBC, and other major banks that dominate the market.

Aldoino points out that the company needs "the best gold trading platform in the world" to continue buying gold in the long term and take advantage of potential market inefficiencies. The company is still analyzing the market, and potential trading strategies will be adjusted so that the company "maintains a large amount of physical gold."

As gold is experiencing its biggest rally since the 1970s, Tether has launched a new product—Tether Gold (XAUT), a gold token redeemable for physical gold bars. Aldoino predicts that XAUT's market circulation by the end of this year is "very likely" to reach $5 billion to $10 billion.

3. Locking in Future Value: Buying BTC

According to Bitinfo Charts data, Tether has become one of the largest active Bitcoin holders, ranking fifth in the number of Bitcoin addresses held by the company, behind only Binance, Robinhood, and Bitfinex.

At the end of 2025, Tether just bought 8888 BTC.

Buying BTC is also part of Tether's quarterly Bitcoin accumulation plan. The company invests up to 15% of its profits in Bitcoin every three months.

III. Tether's Future?

1. Continue to Deepen Stablecoin Development

On January 27, Tether announced the official launch of USA₮, a US federally regulated, dollar-backed stablecoin developed specifically to operate within the new federal stablecoin framework established in the United States under the GENIUS Act.

The issuer of USA₮ is Anchorage Digital Bank, NA, the first federally regulated stablecoin issuer in the United States. Bo Hines, former executive director of the White House Crypto Council, serves as CEO. USA₮ is now open to US users seeking a dollar-backed token designed to operate within the US federal system. With the gradual refinement of the federal regulatory framework, USA₮ enters a market where dollar-backed tokens already play a central role in global commerce. As countries vie to shape the future monetary landscape, the launch of USA₮ further solidifies the dollar's strong position. Tether CEO Aldoino stated, “USA₮ provides institutional investors with an additional option: a token manufactured in the United States and backed by the US dollar. For over a decade, USD₮ has proven that a digital dollar can provide trust, transparency, and utility globally. USA₮ further expands this mission by offering a federally regulated product designed specifically for the US market.” Tether USA₮ CEO Bo Hines noted, “With the launch of USA₮, we see a digital dollar designed to meet federal regulatory expectations. Our focus is on stability, transparency, and responsible governance to ensure the United States continues to lead dollar innovation.” 2. Starting with Stablecoins, But More Than Just Stablecoins Tether’s success is inextricably linked to the current stablecoin boom and the direction of Federal Reserve policy, but Tether is no longer simply a stablecoin issuer—it’s more like a shadow central bank.

1) Digital Dollar

Tether's USDT has achieved a 1:1 peg to the US dollar and is accepted by global cryptocurrency investors, with a circulating supply reaching hundreds of billions of dollars, holding a significant position in the stablecoin market. Even Circle lags far behind: Tether's USDT and Circle's USDC account for approximately 60.07% and 24.66% of the stablecoin market capitalization respectively, a difference of about 35.41%.

Tether's USDT is also widely popular in emerging markets: In the Philippines, Tether's P2P network enables micro-entrepreneurs to conduct near-instant transactions; while in Venezuela, hyperinflation has led to the devaluation of the bolivar, and USDT is used for everything from daily shopping to paying wages. Tether's impact on emerging markets is redefining the role of stablecoins in economic development. By bridging the gaps in digital and physical infrastructure, Tether not only promotes financial inclusion but also lays the foundation for sustainable development in regions historically underserved by traditional financial systems.

Tether is increasingly functioning as a "digital dollar."

2) US Treasury Reserves

As mentioned above, Tether holds a huge amount of US Treasury bonds, creating substantial demand for funds in the US Treasury market.

Tether is a private company, but the scale of its government debt holdings is comparable to that of some countries with large foreign exchange reserves. Furthermore, Tether's treasury reserves are similar in composition to the reserve assets of some sovereign nations: a core of low-risk, highly liquid assets, supplemented by gold and other assets that can preserve value to enhance overall risk resistance.

3) Earning from US Treasury Interest Rates

Tether invests the funds "deposited" by USDT holders in highly liquid US Treasury assets, earning huge amounts of interest during periods of high interest rates. However, it does not pay interest to USDT holders, meaning that almost all the profits go to the company. This allows Tether to profit while also influencing the global US Treasury market and dollar liquidity.

Tether does not pay interest to USDT holders, meaning that almost all profits go to the company.

However, Tether differs significantly from traditional central banks: it is not subject to national sovereign regulation; it does not assume national-level macroeconomic stability responsibilities; and its goal is solely to maintain the stability of USDT and generate profits. 3. If the interest rate environment changes, can Tether still make so much money? Since Trump returned to the White House, he has been urging the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates, repeatedly expressing views such as "a significant and swift rate cut." State Street Investment Management predicts that the Federal Reserve may cut interest rates three times in 2026. As mentioned above, Tether's huge profits are closely related to the Federal Reserve's interest rate policy. So, if the Federal Reserve's interest rate environment changes, can Tether still make so much money? Undoubtedly, when the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, Tether's profits will shrink significantly. Based on its current holdings of $135 billion in US Treasury bonds, a 25 basis point rate cut by the Federal Reserve would reduce Tether's earnings by $337.5 million; a 75 basis point cut would reduce its earnings by $1.0125 billion. Therefore, if subsequent Fed policies change, Tether will likely seek other profit-making opportunities: gold and Bitcoin are among the assets Tether is considering, and its gold-backed stablecoin XAUT is positioning itself for new revenue streams. Compared to its current "passive income" model, Tether may lean more towards investing in riskier assets and providing users with yield-generating financial products, seeking more business opportunities amidst market volatility. Conclusion Tether's ability to achieve annual profits of $15 billion is inextricably linked to its precise grasp of every market opportunity: while maintaining a strong peg to the US dollar, it leverages the expansion of the crypto market to address the shortcomings of emerging market currencies, achieving a "win-win" situation during the Fed's high-interest-rate cycle. However, this period of high profitability is unsustainable. If subsequent Fed policies change, Tether's profit margins will be compressed. Will Tether still be able to earn so much money in 2026? Will it launch other profitable products...? These are questions the industry is currently focusing on. But one thing is certain: Tether has leaped from an ordinary stablecoin company to a significant player in the global financial system, marking the true arrival of the crypto era.

data.title: Stablecoins Could Drain $500B From U.S. Banks by 2028 as Crypto Picks Up Deposits: Standard Chartered Report
data.description:
Regional Banks on the Front Lines

U.S. dollar-backed stablecoins are emerging as a major threat to traditional banking deposits, with a new analysis from Standard Chartered estimating that as much as $500 billion could be siphoned from U.S. bank coffers by the end of 2028 if current adoption trends continue.

The report, led by digital assets research head Geoff Kendrick, paints a stark picture of a financial landscape in which digital tokens begin to displace core banking functions — potentially intensifying the political and regulatory battle over how stablecoins should be governed.

FYI: $500 billion.

💸 That’s how much value stablecoins could pull from U.S. bank deposits by 2028, per Standard Chartered.

🏦 The bank says digital dollars, like USDT & USDC, are moving beyond crypto markets and beginning to pose structural questions for bank funding,… pic.twitter.com/FaCmIYEPBf

— Naga Avan-Nomayo (@JeSuisNaga) January 27, 2026

Standard Chartered’s analysis highlights regional U.S. banks as the institutions most exposed to this emerging risk, largely because their revenue models rely heavily on net interest margin income — the gap between earnings on loans and the interest paid on customer deposits.

As stablecoins grow in popularity for payments, tokenized cash holdings, and alternatives to low-yield savings accounts, that deposit base faces mounting pressure, undermining the traditional funding model that regional lenders depend on.

Unlike diversified or investment banks that earn a smaller portion of revenue from deposit-based lending, regional banks have less cushion to absorb a wave of capital outflows into digital alternatives. If the stablecoin market expands toward its projected multi-trillion-dollar valuation by 2028, roughly one-third of that growth could come directly at the expense of deposits in developed-market banking systems.

Regulatory Loopholes Fuel the Fight

The looming threat comes amid a high-stakes legislative standoff in Washington. Last year, President Donald Trump signed legislation aimed at creating a federal regulatory framework for stablecoins, a move widely expected to accelerate adoption of dollar-pegged digital tokens.

🚨TRUMP: “I’M COMMITTED TO SIGNING LANDMARK CRYPTO MARKET STRUCTURE LEGISLATION THIS YEAR.” 🇺🇸💥

The message is clear — @realDonaldTrump wants full crypto legislation this year, not next. After the stablecoin bill, this could define how the entire U.S. crypto market is regulated… pic.twitter.com/oWuqoBrza0

— Diana (@InvestWithD) November 9, 2025

While the law prohibits stablecoin issuers from paying interest directly on tokens, critics argue it left open a crucial loophole: third parties such as crypto exchanges and decentralized platforms can still offer yield-like incentives through rewards, affiliate programs, or token-based promotions.

Banking groups have warned lawmakers that allowing these inducements could encourage consumers to move savings out of traditional bank accounts and into stablecoins, threatening the deposit base that supports lending to households and businesses. Crypto firms, however, counter that restricting yield mechanisms would unfairly favor banks and stifle innovation in payments and digital finance.

The dispute has already slowed progress on broader crypto market structure legislation, with Senate hearings delayed amid disagreements over how to balance financial stability risks against competition and technological advancement.

One factor amplifying the risk is that the largest stablecoin issuers do not recycle funds back into commercial banks. Major issuers such as Tether and Circle hold only a small share of their reserves as bank deposits, instead favoring U.S. Treasuries and other highly liquid assets. As a result, when money leaves banks to enter stablecoins, it rarely returns to support traditional lending activity.

This creates a largely one-directional flow of capital, reinforcing stablecoins as payment and settlement tools while weakening banks’ role as custodians of everyday money. Over time, this dynamic could further entrench stablecoins as parallel financial infrastructure rather than a complementary extension of the banking system.

Broader Market Implications

The warning adds to a growing chorus of concern from financial leaders who argue that stablecoins could fundamentally reshape capital markets. Some executives have suggested that, under permissive regulatory conditions, trillions of dollars in deposits could theoretically migrate into digital cash equivalents, altering how financial intermediation works.

Beyond deposits, stablecoins are increasingly used as liquidity layers, settlement rails, and cross-border payment tools, replacing services traditionally dominated by banks. This shift is already visible in regions where crypto-enabled payment platforms offer faster and cheaper alternatives to wire transfers and correspondent banking.

Bank of America CEO on why stablecoins shouldn't pay interest:

(TLDR: consumers shouldn't earn yield so banks can)

Quick summary:
Interest on stables -> mass deposit flight
Fully reserved money -> no fractional leverage
Banks lose free funding -> profits go bye bye! https://t.co/WE5P7F6V48pic.twitter.com/2ebBx82NE9

— Omar (@TheOneandOmsy) January 15, 2026

International policymakers have also cautioned that widespread stablecoin adoption could complicate monetary policy and capital controls, forcing governments to rethink how digital assets interact with fiat currencies.

As stablecoin adoption accelerates and regulatory clarity remains elusive, U.S. banks face mounting pressure to adapt. Standard Chartered’s projection of a half-trillion-dollar deposit drain by 2028 underscores the scale of the challenge. Whether lawmakers move to close regulatory loopholes, banks embrace tokenized finance, or stablecoins continue to expand unchecked will shape the future of banking, payments, and digital money in the years ahead.

data.title: Delphi Digital: Five Future Trends in the Crypto Market
data.description:
Author: Delphi Digital; Translator: @Jinse Finance xz

Strategies that worked in previous cycles are no longer applicable. Some sectors of the market are performing exceptionally well, while the overall performance is stagnant. This has led to a significant divergence in investment returns, far removed from the shared profit experience that people are used to expecting.

This reflects both the market's maturation and a deeper structural shift.

1 No longer exclusively favored by capital

For many years, crypto assets have been the default destination for speculative capital seeking venture capital. This situation has now changed.

In 2024, private investment in generative artificial intelligence reached $35 billion, a 7.5-fold increase from 2022. In the first quarter of 2025 alone, robotics startups raised $2 billion. Today, every dollar of new liquidity faces competition from more growth assets available for investment. Crypto assets not only need to compete with other crypto projects, but also with all the cutting-edge technology narratives vying for speculative capital. The data clearly confirms this trend: Over the past 24 months, crypto stocks have outperformed most altcoins. Institutional capital's interest in spot ETFs and stocks like Coinbase, Robinhood, and Galaxy far exceeds its focus on the tokens themselves. Crypto stocks have effectively diverted some of the funding demand that might have flowed to altcoins. 2. The infrastructure layer is becoming commoditized. Meanwhile, the "fat protocol" theory is failing. This theory posits that the infrastructure layer will capture value, while the application layer will remain thin and fungible. This made sense in an era of scarce block space and protocols having pricing power. However, this theory no longer applies when data availability costs approach zero, the execution layer is fragmented by rollups and application chains, and the MEV (Maximum Extractable Value) is stripped away by application layer abstractions attempting to monopolize value. Real economic value is becoming the core metric—that is, the fees actually paid by users and flowing back to token holders (without driving growth through value dilution through issuance). 3. Macroeconomic Cycles and the Crypto Market The liquidity environment that has constrained the development of the crypto market over the past two years is reversing. However, whether the crypto market will respond remains to be seen. After withdrawing $2.4 trillion from the system since 2022, the Federal Reserve stopped quantitative tightening last December. Combined with market pricing already reflecting expectations of interest rate cuts continuing until 2026, this is the first net liquidity positive environment since the beginning of 2022. The Bitcoin/Gold ratio is currently at an all-time low. Gold has broken through +2 standard deviations in liquidity metrics, while Bitcoin's similar metrics are near bear market levels. The monthly relative strength index (RSI) for Bitcoin/Gold is in its most severe oversold condition ever. This weakness can be explained by several factors, including the threat of quantum computing, early whale selling, and the lack of market structure legislation. However, the data reveals a more fundamental mechanism: Bitcoin is currently negatively correlated with gold and Japanese 10-year government bonds, while gold and Japanese government bonds are positively correlated. Both gold and Japanese government bonds have experienced statistically significant fluctuations, deviating from historical norms by more than 2-3 standard deviations. When the market experiences such extreme volatility, the correlation pattern inevitably indicates that the related assets will exhibit abnormally weak performance. Bitcoin consistently lags behind gold. Once the situation regarding Japanese government bonds eases, and the Bank of Japan and the Federal Reserve find a way to stabilize carry trades, the pressure on gold and commodities will be alleviated—this may be Bitcoin's true window of opportunity. Stablecoins have become a core application scenario. Stablecoins have developed into the largest application scenario in the crypto space. Adjusted monthly stablecoin trading volume has surpassed PayPal and Visa. The total supply of stablecoins has exceeded $304 billion, a 33% increase compared to last year. Its reserves hold approximately $133 billion in U.S. Treasury bonds, making stablecoins the 19th largest holder of U.S. Treasury securities. The entire payment system is therefore being compressed. We are moving from the old world of card organizations, acquiring banks, issuing banks, and settlement cycles to a new era of programmable on-chain settlement. Stablecoins eliminate intermediary steps, simplify reconciliation processes, and internalize compliance as a native function rather than an afterthought. 5. Value Migrating Towards the Application Layer As infrastructure becomes commoditized and liquidity returns, value may flow to the application layer, which controls user relationships. Coinbase, Robinhood, Binance, and Hyperliquid are all heading towards the same destination from different starting points: the aggregation layer. The crypto space has a structural advantage in this regard. When Block (formerly Square) wanted to launch its "buy now, pay later" business, it had to spend $29 billion to acquire Afterpay. A crypto super app, however, only needs to integrate Aave to provide lending services and route perpetual contract transactions through Hyperliquid, earning a share through the builder code—without incurring acquisition costs. Leading fintech companies are taking a further step. Instead of leasing block space from existing public chains, they are building their own dedicated chains. Stripe launched the Tempo chain earlier this year, optimized for payment speed and using native stablecoins to pay gas fees; Robinhood is also following a similar path by building its own L2 chain for equity tokenization. In the past, on-chain activity lacked diverse scenarios beyond directional token speculation. Now, ecosystems such as prediction markets (via Polymarket), on-chain lending, and deep perpetual contract liquidity have taken shape. All functions that meet an individual's financial needs can be accessed through a single interface. Real-world asset tokenization is a key driver of this process. In January 2025, the value of on-chain tokenized stocks was approximately $15 million; it has now surpassed $500 million. Anyone can hold equity assets through a self-custodied wallet without a brokerage account. The development of equity perpetual contracts is even more rapid. Platforms such as Hyperliquid, Ostium Labs, and VestExchange now offer leveraged exposure to stocks, indices, commodities, and forex. CFD brokers generate hundreds of billions of dollars in daily trading volume outside the US, and retail options flows increasingly reflect purely directional bets rather than volatility trading—perpetual contracts are a purer tool for meeting both of these needs. As a result, brokerage accounts are quietly being replaced by self-custodied wallets. Stocks, cryptocurrencies, forex, and commodities coexist on a single interface, with settlement, margining, and custody all operating on crypto infrastructure. The era of passively allocating "crypto assets" as an asset class is over. Bitcoin is emerging as a self-contained macro asset, decoupling from the crypto market. Protocols that truly capture fees are consistently outperforming the market; narratives alone are no longer sufficient. Stablecoin infrastructure is becoming the bridge connecting the crypto world to everything else.

data.title: YouTube star Dan Koe's long post: How to fix your life in one day
data.description:
Author: Annie Mei  Image: Dan Koe's Twitter image

Recently, I've been researching some independent thinkers, and Dan Koe is one of the representatives I greatly admire.

He has 1.26 million subscribers on YouTube and 790,000 followers on Twitter (X).

In just four years, he went from earning $3,000 a month to $4.2 million a year. His success is not an unattainable myth, but a path that every ordinary person can learn from.

He just sent a tweet today, which impressed me very much:

The best thing you can do in today's world is practice thinking.

Very few people, especially smart people, do this.

Snap yourself out of your automatic patterns and stretch your mind for once.

This will only become more important as AI and social media distort reality.

Translation is:

I. The reason you're not in the place you want to go is because you're not yet "the person who will be there."

Most people, when they think of change, their first reaction is:Change actions → Set goals → Force self-discipline.

The result is often: relying on motivation for a few weeks, and then returning to square one.

Dan said: Changing your goals isn't the most important thing; changing "who you are" is.

Dan said:Changing your goals isn't the most important thing; changing "who you are" is.

For example: Do bodybuilders need to "force" themselves to maintain a healthy diet? Do CEOs need to "force" themselves to take responsibility? They don't feel pain because that's their lifestyle and identity. Dan is often described as "too disciplined," and even his mother tells him, "You should relax." His answer is: If I don't find it interesting, why should I do what I'm doing? If you want to achieve a certain result, you must begin living a life that will produce that result before you even arrive at it. If you want health, start living a healthy life now; if you want a certain kind of life, start living that identity now. Secondly, the reason you're not going to the places you want to go is because you don't actually want to go there. This statement sounds counterintuitive at first glance. "I clearly want to be rich, how could it be that I don't?" Dan explained this from the perspective of "brain operation mechanisms." In *The Miracle Power of Mind-Body Unity*, Galway proposed a concept: the self has two levels. Self 1: Consciousness, mind, reason; Self 2: Body, subconscious, instinct. Often, it is Self 1 that wants to go there, while Self 2 resists. For example, procrastination. The apparent reason is "I lack self-discipline," but the real goal may be: to avoid the risks of being judged, criticized, or noticed.

Third, the reason you're not going to the places you want to go is because you're afraid of actually getting there

Dan gives a very important "identity formation cycle":

  1. You want to achieve a goal

  2. You see the world through the "lens" of this goal

  3. You only pay attention to information (learning) that is useful to the goal

  4. Act and get feedback

data.title: On-chain is not a tax haven: CARF closes in, launching a global tax crackdown on crypto assets.
data.description:
Author: danny; Source: X, @agintender

Who would have thought that the modern multinational tax information system was caused by a tube of toothpaste? A UBS banker smuggled diamonds across the border by stuffing them into a toothpaste tube—a scene reminiscent of Hollywood—unexpectedly tolled the death knell for Swiss banking secrecy laws. Now, the gears of history are relentlessly grinding towards the crypto world—that once-hidden "tax haven" is about to face its reckoning.

This article will unveil the mystery of CARF: it's the closing net of a global tax operation. From Binance's strategic move to relocate to the UAE to buy time, to the harsh reality that "crypto-to-crypto trading" is no longer tax-free; from Hong Kong's compliance countdown to the shattering of mainland investors' wishful thinking.

... This is not only a reshaping of the industry landscape, but also a survival guide that every crypto asset holder must face—after all, in this algorithm-woven cage, no one can continue to bury their head in the sand like an ostrich. Introduction: What is CARF? CARF stands for Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework. Its core mechanism is that Crypto Asset Service Providers (RCASPs) with reporting obligations collect tax-related information about their clients and related transactions, and submit it to the tax authorities in their respective jurisdictions. Ultimately, this information is automatically exchanged internationally between tax authorities. This is similar to CRS in the traditional financial sector, but CARF specifically focuses on the buying, selling, exchanging, custody, and transfer of crypto assets. Simply put, previously, when users traded cryptocurrencies on exchanges, the tax authorities of their country of residence had difficulty fully grasping the relevant information. CARF now connects users' tax residency countries with the jurisdictions of exchanges. Once a CARF partnership is established, the user's tax residency country can obtain detailed information about its tax residents' overseas cryptocurrency trading activities and conduct tax collection accordingly. As of the end of 2025, more than 75 jurisdictions have committed to implementing CARF in 2027 or 2028, with over half having signed relevant competent authority agreements. From January 1, 2026, the CARF framework will be implemented in the first batch of 48 jurisdictions, covering the UK, EU, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, and other regions.

Chapter 1, Diamonds in Toothpaste, The End of Secrecy and the Arrival of CRS

To understand CARF's "new scythe," we must first look at the "old fishing net"—CRS (Common Reporting Standard).

The protagonist of the story is Bradley Birkenfeld, formerly a major account manager at UBS.

To smuggle $200 million in untaxed assets belonging to his client, American real estate tycoon Igor Olenicoff, from UBS back to the United States without leaving a trace, Birkenfeld devised a plot only a Hollywood screenwriter would dare to use: he purchased diamonds, stuffed them into a tube of ordinary toothpaste to evade customs X-rays, and then brazenly flew across the Atlantic to deliver the diamonds to Olenicoff for liquidation. In 2007, when Birkenfeld discovered he might become a scapegoat for an internal compliance purge in a bank report, he made a decision that defied the traditions of Swiss banking: he defected. He walked into the door of the U.S. Department of Justice with a list of top-secret internal emails and client names. Birkenfeld's testimony directly led to UBS paying a record-breaking $780 million fine in 2009 and unprecedentedly handing over a list of more than 4,000 American clients. This marked the death of Swiss bank secrecy laws. (Interestingly, Birkenfeld ultimately took home a bounty of 104 million.) The US Congress realized that relying on informants like Birkenfeld was far from sufficient; an automated monitoring mechanism was necessary. Thus, in 2010, the most authoritative tax law in history, the Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA), was enacted. Its logic was simple and brutal: "Banks worldwide that want to do business with the United States must report the account balances of Americans to us annually." Seeing the immediate effectiveness of this US approach, the OECD began to replicate it. In 2014, the global standard based on FATCA—the Common Reporting Standard (CRS)—was officially born. This is why the underlying logic of CRS is very similar to checking bank statements: it assumes that wealth will eventually accumulate in bank accounts, generating interest and forming a balance. It's a monitoring system tailor-made for the "fiat currency era," aiming to expose hidden millionaires through an annual "balance snapshot." Just as everything was moving in the direction regulators hoped for, a new phenomenon called Bitcoin was quietly emerging. This CRS system, based on "balance monitoring," was about to face a completely new adversary it had never imagined. Chapter Two: The Vulnerabilities of the Old Hunting Net—Why CARF When CRS Exists? Using an AI analogy, CARF is like a high-definition camera installed 24/7 at the entrance of every compliant exchange. Its biggest difference from CRS is that CRS checks "how much money you have," while CARF checks "where you've spent your money." 2.1 The Origin and Strategic Intent of CARF The birth of CARF stemmed from the G20 countries' fear of tax base erosion. While the traditional CRS has been effective in combating offshore tax evasion, it primarily targets traditional bank accounts and custodian accounts. Crypto assets, due to their decentralized nature and peer-to-peer transfer capabilities without intermediaries, have become a blind spot for CRS. The OECD has explicitly stated that CARF aims to eliminate this blind spot by including crypto asset service providers (CASPs) in the same information reporting obligations as banks. By the end of 2025, more than 50 jurisdictions (including the UK, Canada, France, Germany, Japan, and the Cayman Islands) have committed to implementing CARF. This framework quietly began data collection in the Cayman Islands and other locations on January 1, 2026, and will conduct its first information exchange in 2027. 2.2 Comparison of CARF and CRS 2.0: From "Stock" to "Circulation" The core logic of CRS is to monitor "stock wealth," while the core logic of CARF is to monitor the circulation of wealth. Under the CRS framework, apart from the year-end balance, the tax authorities can hardly see the intermediate processes. However, under CARF, if an investor converts Bitcoin into USDT, transfers USDT to their cold wallet, or even uses cryptocurrency to purchase more than $50,000 worth of $PUNDIAI (retail payment transactions), each action will generate a report record. CARF effectively elevates the perspective from a "static balance sheet" to a "dynamic cash flow statement."

2.3 Scope of “Related Crypto Assets”

CARF’s definition of “related crypto assets” covers almost all crypto assets:

Stablecoins: Although many stablecoins claim to be alternatives to fiat currency, under CARF, they are explicitly considered crypto assets. This means that an exchange between USDT and USD may no longer be considered a “currency exchange” but a transaction, and such a transaction is a taxable event.

NFTs: While CARF primarily focuses on assets used for payments or investments, most high-value NFTs are likely to be included in the reporting scope due to their secondary market trading attributes. Tokenized Securities: Even tokenized stocks or bonds already regulated in traditional financial markets, even if they are on-chain, may be subject to both CRS and CARF coverage (although the OECD has attempted to avoid duplicate reporting by revising CRS, this overlap is difficult to avoid due to the principle of "better to err on the side of caution" in tax practice). Chapter 3. Retail Investors' Sentimentality, Wishful Thinking, and Devastation 3.1 Crypto-to-Crypto Trading: A Mandatory "Fair Price" Mechanism CARF stipulates that all exchanges between crypto assets must record their fair market value in fiat currency at the moment the transaction occurs. In the eyes of the tax authorities, "crypto-to-crypto trading" is equivalent to "selling before buying." A common misconception is: "If I exchange Bitcoin for Ethereum, as long as I don't convert it to fiat currency (USD/CNY), it doesn't count as a sale, and I don't need to pay tax." But this is just wishful thinking on the part of novice investors. CARF requires exchanges to record: "On a certain day, Zhang San exchanged 1 Bitcoin for 20 Ethereum. At that time, 1 Bitcoin was worth $50,000." In the eyes of the tax authorities, this is a taxable event of "selling Bitcoin for $50,000." Even though you don't have cash on hand, your tax bill has already been generated. CARF has completely ended the tax avoidance strategy of "using cryptocurrency to generate more cryptocurrency." After 2026 (2027 in some regions), every cryptocurrency exchange will be recorded as an asset disposal event, leaving a definite "fiat currency gain record" in your tax file, regardless of whether you convert it into fiat currency/stablecoin. 3.2 Penetrating Wallets: Transaction Hash and Address Cleaning In CARF's XML Schema, RCASP is required to report the specific type and value of transactions. Although the final rule, after strong lobbying from the industry, removed the mandatory requirement to report all non-custodial wallet receiving addresses, the internal system collects and retains information about this address and its associated beneficiaries for at least 5 years (aka the "retention rule"). This means that the tax authorities have the right to access the data at any time. If the tax authorities find that a taxpayer has a large "withdrawal" record in 2026 but has not declared subsequent income, they can send bulk information requests to exchanges to accurately obtain these external wallet addresses. When you withdraw coins from an exchange to your own wallet plugin or cold wallet, the exchange must record and report (if requested) "which address was withdrawn". This is similar to withdrawing cash from a bank. The bank not only records how much you withdrew but also tracks you and notes which safe you put the money in. Once your wallet address and your real identity are linked in the tax bureau's database, all your DeFi operations on the blockchain are essentially "naked." 3.3 Standardization of Valuation Anchoring What if the transaction involves two extremely obscure cryptocurrencies (e.g., exchanging "air coin A" for "air coin B"), and there's no fiat currency trading pair? CARF stipulates a "cascading valuation method": if asset A has no fiat currency price, the fiat currency price of asset B is referenced; if neither exists, the service provider must use a reasonable valuation method to enforce pricing based on this. In short, the system must generate a fiat currency value and send it to the tax bureau. This eliminates the possibility for users to use price fluctuations to make vague declarations when filing taxes. 3.4 Mandatory Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN) CARF requires RCASP to collect users' tax residency and corresponding Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN). However, if a user only declares their tax residency in a lower-tax jurisdiction (such as Dubai), but the exchange discovers through IP address, area code, or login logs that they frequently operate in a higher-tax jurisdiction (such as France), the exchange has an obligation to question the legitimacy of this self-certification. Chapter 4, The Trap of Retrospection: 2026 as the "Year of Exposure" Many veteran investors believe that as long as they handle their assets before the first information exchange in 2027, everything will be fine. This is incorrect. Everyone overlooks CARF's "retrospective effect," meaning that the 2027 information exchange implies submitting information from 2026. 4.1 “Beginning Balance” and Historical Audit When the Inland Revenue Department (IRD) receives the CARF data for the entire year of 2026 in 2027, they will first focus on the “Beginning Balance” or “Total Annual Transactions.” Scenario Simulation: Assume that Mr. Nakamoto, a Chinese investor, sold $10 million worth of $PUNDIAI tokens through a compliant Hong Kong platform in 2026. The platform reports the data to the IRD based on CARF data. The IRD's AI system will immediately compare Mr. Nakamoto's personal income tax returns from 2025 onwards. If Mr. Nakamoto has never previously declared holding overseas crypto assets, the source of this $10 million becomes a huge question. The IRD will use the hash value of this transaction to trace back to when these $PUNDIAI tokens were purchased. If the purchase was made in 2024, then all unreported capital gains between 2024 and 2026 will be exposed. It's worth noting that tax authorities in many countries have deployed AI-based big data analytics systems specifically designed to identify anomalies where asset holdings don't match reported income. We anticipate a major tax evasion crisis for crypto millionaires in 2026. 4.2 The Compliance Window in 2026 For investors who haven't yet complied, 2026 is effectively the last window of opportunity. Before the data gates close, investors face a difficult choice: Proactively reporting historical assets to the tax authorities can usually lead to a reduction or exemption of penalties. Under a compliant framework (such as family trusts or offshore companies), reorganize asset holdings or seek assistance from professional financial and tax institutions to rationally plan crypto assets. (Advertisement here, ad space is hotly tendered~) Chapter 5, Behind Binance's Relocation: Trading Space for Time Among a host of regulatory-friendly jurisdictions, why did Binance ultimately choose Abu Dhabi? Besides local policy support and advantages in funding channels, another important factor is the **compliance time difference**. Binance's original location in the Cayman Islands is among the first jurisdictions to commit to implementing CARF, with the first information exchange expected in 2027. This means that Crypto Service Providers (RCASPs) with CARF reporting obligations will need to collect and retain information for reporting starting in 2026. If Binance remains based in the Cayman Islands, it must immediately begin building a comprehensive CARF compliance system. Conversely, the UAE, according to the CARF implementation timeline, is among the second batch of jurisdictions to implement CARF and plans to begin information exchange in 2028. From the Cayman Islands to the UAE, Binance has secured a one-year strategic buffer period. For Binance, which serves over 300 million users, this period is of great significance: First, it mitigates early adopter risks. By observing how the first implementing jurisdictions like the UK and the Cayman Islands operate, Binance can learn from the experiences of other exchanges and optimize its own compliance strategy. Second, it allows participation in rule-making. Currently, the UAE's CARF legislation and implementation details are still being formulated. As a leading exchange with considerable influence, Binance has the opportunity to voice its opinions, consult with authorities, and exert a positive influence on the formation of localized rules. Third, it allows for system upgrades. This year provides Binance with ample time to deploy and debug a data reporting and management system that meets the complex requirements of CARF. This is what is known as "trading space for time." Chapter 6. CARF in China: Impact and Trends As one of the world's largest markets for crypto asset users, China's situation is somewhat unique. Some say that mainland China is not among the first signatories of the OECD's CARF, so mainland tax authorities cannot see cryptocurrency transactions conducted in Hong Kong—this is actually a misunderstanding. Mainland China has not yet joined or committed to implementing CARF, therefore mainland tax authorities will not obtain crypto asset transaction data of Chinese tax residents based on the CARF mechanism. However, this does not mean that mainland crypto tycoons can rest easy. Besides, mainland China is already an active participant in CRS. Although CARF targets crypto assets, if crypto assets are converted into fiat currency and deposited in banks, or held in the form of financial assets (such as ETFs), they are already within the CRS monitoring network. Furthermore, the consultation document also mentions that CARF information will be exchanged with "partner jurisdictions." Attentive readers will notice that Hong Kong is in the second tier of implementing CARF, having already initiated legislative consultations on CARF and CRS revisions and formulated a clear implementation roadmap, planning to complete legislative preparations in 2027 and begin information exchange in 2028. Under the "dual-track" crypto regulatory framework, the impact of CARF's implementation in China needs to be considered in different ways: Crypto users with Hong Kong identities are obligated to submit self-certified information to exchanges under the CARF framework. Subsequently, their crypto asset transaction data on overseas exchanges will be reported and exchanged with the Hong Kong tax authorities through an automatic exchange mechanism. This means increased transparency in assets and transactions, making it more difficult for users to evade tax obligations by leveraging the decentralized and anonymous nature of crypto transactions. Simultaneously, Hong Kong crypto exchanges, as RCASPs (Regulatory Service Providers), must strengthen KYC (Know Your Customer) requirements and establish data collection and reporting systems in accordance with CARF requirements. Failure to register, declare, conduct due diligence, or submit inaccurate information at any stage can trigger legal liability, with fines reaching millions of Hong Kong dollars. In contrast, the impact of CARF on mainland China in the short term is relatively limited. This is related to the mainland's "illegal" characterization of crypto assets. However, transparency in crypto taxation is an inevitable trend, and mainland Chinese tax residents cannot be complacent. With Hong Kong's access to CARF's global information exchange network, it is possible that mainland China will obtain relevant crypto transaction data from Hong Kong through other channels, or join CARF in the future. For mainland Chinese investors, the era of relying on Hong Kong as a "safe haven" is over. Although there may be a time lag of several years for automatic exchange, the "on-demand exchange" channel is open, and data retention rules ensure that historical records are readily accessible. Chapter 7, Survival Guide – Don't Be an Ostrich Burying Its Head in the Sand. If you ask a Korean oppa what the three unavoidable things in this world are: life and death, Samsung (a Korean entertainment brand), and taxes. As individuals caught in this tide of the times, what should we do? **Pay Attention to the Tax Consequences of Cryptocurrency Trading:** Don't naively think that you don't have to pay taxes if you don't withdraw funds. From now on, every click of "buy/sell" carries the potential for taxation. (In countries with capital gains tax) **Clean Up Your Accounts:** Clean up those "zombie accounts" on unknown small exchanges or registered with random identities. Either cancel them or withdraw your coins. Once the CARF network is fully operational, these accounts will be among the first to be subject to risk control. Understanding Cold Wallets: Cold wallets remain your last line of defense for your data, but the bridge between them is now under surveillance. When you transfer funds from Binance to a cold wallet, the transaction itself becomes a record. While the tax authorities cannot see everything in a cold wallet, they know: "This address belongs to Nakamoto Murao, who transferred 10 bitcoins into it in 2027." Pay Attention to the UAE and Hong Kong Timelines: The UAE and Hong Kong are among the second batch of regions to implement the policy (swap in 2028). This means you have roughly a year or two to adapt and plan. Using this time to learn how to comply with regulations or to find a professional tax advisor is far more practical than searching for the next "tax haven."

......

Postscript

This article thanks FinTax for their professional analysis of tax regulations and their observations and analyses of various jurisdictions, which enriched the practical perspective of this article.

data.title: RWA Outlook 2026: Macroeconomics, Liquidity, and Market Reach
data.description:
Author: CoinFound & DigiFT

Overview

2025 was a breakthrough year for the tokenization of Real World Assets (RWAs), marking the space's transition from pilot phases to early institutional scale. Issuers significantly expanded the types of tokenized products, including money market funds, private lending, commodities, and emerging equity tokenization models; meanwhile, the total value locked in on-chain RWAs continued to rise.
